text: Robotic anterior exoneration in females. Tips and tricks.
Radical cystectomy is the gold standard for treatment of muscle invasive bladder cancer.

Females are less likely than men to experience bladder cancer by a factor of four to one and therefore less frequently undergo radical cystectomy. Generally, urologists are less familiar with female pelvic anatomy and are more comfortable with radical cystectomy in men than women.

With the increased utilization of robotic radical cystectomy, females are potentially able to benefit even more with a truly minimally invasive operation. as the specimen can be extracted through the vagina, resulting in no fascial closure.
From September, to October, we have performed seventy-five robotic radical cystectomies, with ten being in females.
In the following video, we demonstrate and highlight some tips and tricks for robotic anterior exoneration in females.
The surgeon places ports similarly to male patients and identifies the gonadal veins bilaterally, preparing for ligation at the highest possible point to ensure a single vein remains.
The surgeon dissects the gonadal vein caudally, revealing its anatomical path leading towards the ovaries.

The surgeon dissects the ureters as caudally as possible, observing their path crossing underneath the uterine arteries. The uterine arteries are then ligated in preparation for a subsequent hysterectomy.
The surgeon grasps the uterus, fallopian tubes, ovaries, and round ligaments using surgical instruments, preparing for further dissection or removal.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneal reflection posterior to the broad ligament, exposing the ureters beneath.
The broad ligament is visible, containing the round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovary. The surgeon uses the fallopian tubes as a handle to lift the uterus upward.
A sponge stick is inserted into the vagina to aid in identifying the cervix, ensuring careful placement to avoid accidental insertion into the rectum.
The surgeon incises the vagina using electrocautery, with the cervix positioned anteriorly. The dissection reveals significant bleeding due to the vascularization of the vaginal tissue.
The surgeon clips the ureters as distally as possible, ensuring they are securely occluded before proceeding with further dissection.

The surgeon cleans the lateral sidewalls to expose the endopelvic fascia, extending the dissection laterally and cephalad to ensure maximum tissue removal. The lateral perivesical tissue is visibly separated and prepared for immediate removal to prevent obstruction during the rest of the operation.

The surgeon exposes the endopelvic fascia on the right side, removing extra tissue. The uterus is elevated to reveal the vaginal wall, and a vessel sealer device is used to transect the lateral pedicles.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to perform a partial vaginotomy, taking the lateral pedicles low without risk of rectal injury. The vaginal wall is visible medially during this procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer, rolling it medially to maximize the sparing of vaginal tissue during the dissection.
The surgeon drops the bladder in the standard fashion, performing anterior dissection to expose the dorsal venous complex.

With the bladder dropped, the surgeon performs the remainder of the partial vaginectomy from an anterior view. The dorsal venous complex is identified, cauterized using bipolar cautery, and then dissected out.
The surgeon pulls the catheter to identify the bladder neck, aiding in the identification of the urethra. An incision is made just lateral to the urethra, which is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully creates a plane between the urethra and the anterior vaginal wall, ensuring the urethra is circumferentially dissected. The final section of the anterior vaginal wall is dissected, maintaining a safe distance from the urethral dissection to facilitate the creation of a vaginal flap.

The surgeon performs a total urethrectomy by pulling the urethra into the body and carefully dissecting around it in the appropriate plane. After complete transection, a clip is placed on the Foley catheter to maintain the balloon, and the catheter is brought in. The separate urethral meatus and anterior vaginal flap are visible, and the urethra is inspected to confirm the completion of the urethrectomy.

The surgeon places an endocatch bag through the vagina and inserts the surgical specimen into the bag for retrieval. The assistant removes the entire endocatch bag and specimen through the vaginal opening, eliminating the need for a fascial incision or closure.

The surgeon carefully separates the posterior vagina from the posterior peritoneum, ensuring the rectum is not injured. A peritoneal flap is created to cover the vaginal closure. The vagina is closed in a clamshell fashion using two separate zero V-lock sutures, with the anterior flap aiding in the closure.

The surgeon runs the suture from the lateral aspect of each side back to the middle, tying the sutures together. A 3-0 V-lock suture is used to close the separate urethral opening. The peritoneal flap is then positioned to assist in closing the vaginal suture line, aiming to prevent fistula formation.
The surgeon sews the peritoneal flap laterally to the arcus tendineus on both sides, completing the dissection with the peritoneal flap visibly in place.

We completed ten cases successfully without conversion to open. Mean operative time, skin-to-skin, not just consult time, was six hours and fourteen minutes. Mean EBL was three hundred and fifty-one cc's and median length of stay was five days.

Robotic anterior exoneration in females is safely and effectively performed robotically and has the potential extra advantage of not requiring a fascial closure since the specimen can be extracted vaginally.
